Q1: I tried to keep things fairly consistent, usually studying in the evenings after my part-time job. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend time making diagrams and color-coded notes from the lecture videos. With only about 12 hours a week to dedicate to studying, I had to be strategic about what I focused on. Honestly, my routine was more about fitting study time around everything else in my life rather than having a perfect schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I'd try to maintain some regularity, but when deadlines approached, everything intensified. Some weeks I barely touched anything because work was demanding or I had other responsibilities. Being low on resources means I sometimes had to prioritize earning money over studying, which threw off any consistent rhythm I tried to build.
Q3: Usually it was checking if new assignments were posted or seeing if there were updates I'd missed. I logged in pretty frequently actually—43 times over the course—mostly to watch videos or see what discussions were happening. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was staying connected to the course, even if I wasn't actively working on something specific. The LMS became my main window into what was going on.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS in the morning before work and again in the evening. The peer support group I joined really helped with that—knowing others were active made me want to stay engaged too. It became part of my daily routine, like checking email. Having those discussion posts to respond to gave me a reason to keep coming back regularly.
Q5: The lecture videos were absolutely essential for me—I watched over 160 minutes of them because I really need to see concepts visually to understand them. I'd pause, rewind, and sketch out what was being explained. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing how my peers interpreted things helped fill gaps in my understanding. When I was confused, seeing someone else's explanation often clicked better than the original material.
Q6: I tended to skip the really dense reading materials, especially PDFs that were just walls of text. As a visual learner, those didn't work for my brain and I'd zone out. I also didn't engage much with some of the supplementary resources because I simply didn't have time with my limited study hours. If something wasn't directly connected to an assignment, it often got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. I also valued anything that helped me visualize complex concepts—diagrams, videos with animations, that sort of thing. If I could tell it would help my grade or genuinely help me understand something I was struggling with, I'd prioritize it. Time is precious when you're juggling everything I'm juggling.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional stuff—even if interesting—often got sacrificed when time was tight. There was always this mental calculation of 'will this actually help my grade?' With low motivation some days, the optional activities just didn't make the cut. I wish I'd had more bandwidth for them.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and videos to study on the bus to work since I don't always have reliable internet access. I'd print out diagrams at the library and annotate them by hand—that's actually where a lot of my real learning happened. I also used YouTube videos to supplement topics that weren't clicking. A lot of my study time was completely invisible to the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper for hours, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd download a video once and watch it three or four times offline. My peer study group sometimes met in person at the library, and we'd work through material together. The LMS only saw me download the file once, not the hours I spent with it afterward.
Q11: Yes, constantly. With my limited resources and inconsistent internet, I made it a habit to download everything I might need in one session. Then I'd study from those materials for days without logging back in. One video might show as a single view, but I'd have watched it multiple times on my phone offline. It was the most efficient way to work with my situation.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My work schedule picked up, and I was exhausted all the time. There were a couple of weeks where I could barely make it to class—my attendance dropped significantly. I felt this surprising wave of anxiety about falling behind, even though my stress levels were usually manageable. The peer support helped pull me back in, but those were rough weeks.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired from work, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor—I had to work to pay bills, and that always came first. Sometimes confusion about complex topics made me avoid certain modules because I didn't have the energy to wrestle with them. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it should have been.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while doing other things, not really watching. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without actually reading them carefully. Sometimes I was just too mentally exhausted to engage meaningfully, but I'd still log in because I felt like I should be doing something. The activity was there, but my brain wasn't fully present.
Q15: I postponed studying for the exam way too long because the material felt overwhelming and I wasn't confident I understood it. There was this avoidance thing where I'd do assignments—which I was good at—instead of reviewing for tests. I knew exam prep was important, but assignments felt more manageable and gave me immediate feedback. Looking at my exam score now, I really regret that choice.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated everything. I'd see an assignment due date and work backward from there, cramming in the days before. My LMS activity probably spiked right before things were due. Without a deadline, I'd often let things slide because other life demands felt more urgent. The structure of due dates was both helpful and stressful—it kept me accountable but also made everything feel rushed.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep going. My assignment scores were pretty solid, which surprised me and made me feel capable. But the disconnect between my assignment performance and exam performance was confusing and a bit demoralizing. The peer support intervention really helped my mood—having people check in on me made a difference in staying engaged.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but the gap between assignment work and exam preparation wasn't clear to me. I did well on assignments because they were broken into manageable pieces, but the exam required synthesizing everything differently. More guidance on how to prepare for exams specifically would have helped. The online components were flexible, which I needed, but maybe too flexible for someone with my low motivation.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched how I learn best. The discussion forums were good for feeling connected. But the heavy reading requirements were harder for me—I'd get lost in text-heavy materials. Also, the exam format didn't seem to match what we practiced in assignments, which made it harder to know if I was actually prepared. That mismatch really hurt me.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's genuinely trying to engage and learn but struggling to translate that into submitted work. Or maybe they're confused about what's expected and spending time on the wrong things. High activity might mean someone is searching for understanding but not finding it. It could also be someone dealing with anxiety who keeps checking things without being able to complete them.
Q21: That makes sense to me. If someone has good external resources or studies effectively offline, they might not need to be in the LMS constantly. I think I'm somewhere in between—my LMS activity was high, but a lot of my actual learning happened offline. Someone could absolutely master material through textbooks or study groups and only use the LMS for submissions.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they're clicking on, how often they're engaging. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't see me pausing a video to take detailed notes or struggling through a concept on paper. They definitely miss offline studying and the emotional experience of learning. Numbers don't capture understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. For me, it meant making those visual notes, discussing with peers, really trying to grasp concepts even when they were difficult. True engagement is when you're thinking about the course even when you're not logged in, connecting ideas in your head.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled financially and how that affected my energy for studying. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials or the support I got from my peer group outside the platform. It can't see that I was genuinely trying despite my circumstances, or that the peer intervention actually improved my mood significantly. My effort was real, even if my exam score doesn't reflect it.
